text: The surgical team performs a robotic spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy using the DaVinci XI robot. A peri-umbilical camera port provides visualization, while a right lateral port is used for retraction with a fenestrated tip-up grasper. A left lateral abdominal port accommodates a fenestrated grasper, and a left mid-abdominal port is utilized for robotic stapling and vessel sealing. An additional five millimeter assistance port in the left lower quadrant aids in suction and exposure.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic omentum to reveal the pancreas in the retroperitoneum. The dissection extends laterally to the lowest short gastric vessels and medially to expose the pancreas proximal to the pancreatic mass. The inferior border of the pancreas is lifted, and dissection continues laterally towards the tail, exposing the tumor on the inferior aspect of the tail posteriorly. The periopancreatic plane dissection lifts the tumor and tail away from the retroperitoneum, eventually exposing the splenic vein along the superior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon dissects along the superior border of the pancreas, separating the splenic vein from the pancreas both proximally and distally. Tributaries of the splenic vein to the tail of the pancreas are sealed using a vessel sealer. The pancreas is dissected away from the hilum of the spleen inferiorly, taking some vascular supply to the inferior spleen. Medial dissection exposes the distal inferior branch of the splenic vein, with further dissection occurring anterior and posterior to this branch to remove the distal pancreas along with the specimen.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the pancreatic tissue, preserving the splenic venous and arterial branches. The dissection is performed both anteriorly and posteriorly to the distal splenic vessels, lifting the entire pancreatic tail out with the specimen. The tail of the pancreas, extending behind the splenic vein, is dissected and transected using the vessel sealer.

The splenic vein is visible, split into two branches, with the inferior branch positioned along the tail of the pancreas. The surgeon transects distal to the tail of the pancreas, fully mobilizing it. Small attachments to the inferior pole of the spleen are severed using a vessel sealer. The specimen is transected with a blue loaded stapler, leaving a cuff of normal pancreas proximal to the splenic tumor. The specimen is placed in an endo-catch bag and extracted through the expanded stapling port.
A XIX Blake drain is placed through a left lateral abdominal incision, with the spleen appearing well vascularized at the completion of the procedure.
